chunk_summary: Pancreatic insufficiency requires high-calorie, unrestricted fat diets with pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) tailored to clinical response. Proper dosing and administration are critical to avoid complications like fibrosing colonopathy.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) in managing pancreatic insufficiency.
Learn appropriate PERT dosing guidelines for different age groups and meal types.
Recognize the importance of monitoring clinical response and avoiding excessive PERT dosing.
Identify common nutritional deficiencies associated with pancreatic insufficiency, such as fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies.
key_facts: Pancreatic insufficiency requires high-calorie intake with unrestricted fat and PERT.
PERT dosing for infants: 2,000–4,000 lipase units per 120 mL formula or per breastfeed.
PERT dosing for older children: 500–2,500 lipase units/kg/meal and 250–1,250 units/kg/snack.
Doses >6,000 units/kg/meal are linked to fibrosing colonopathy and should be avoided.
Fecal pancreatic elastase-1 <100 mg/g is a practical screening marker for pancreatic insufficiency.
Enzymes must be taken with every fat-containing meal or snack for efficacy.
reference_quote: Write PERT like a drug and titrate to clinical response (improved stool quality and weight trajectory) rather than escalating indefinitely.
document_key_topics: Pancreatic insufficiency and its nutritional management
Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) dosing guidelines
Screening and monitoring for pancreatic insufficiency
